public.title: The Gilt Cage
public.tagline: The soprano hit her high C at eight and was dead of it by ten. The atomizer on her vanity sang the last note.
public.author: rd_case_desk
public.question: Who poisoned Lilya Marchetti's throat spray?

lines.text: Deceased: Marchetti, Lilya, 41, soprano. Collapsed in Act II of THE GILT CAGE at the Regina Opera House. Cause: prussic acid, administered by throat atomizer.
Note for the gallery press: the poison was in the spray, not the wine, not the flowers, not the understudy's eyes.
House rule 11: no visitor passes the corridor mirror during an interval without the doorman's chalk mark.
Statement of A. Wren (public record, excerpt): 'I looked in on Lilya at the interval to wish her a strong second act, as any friend of the house might.'
Company sentiment runs hard against the understudy. Sentiment does not hold an atomizer.
Cloak-room note: checked articles are returned against brass tags; the girl remembers coats by their weight.
Sundry: a diva's superstitions are filed under standing orders, same as the fire rules.

suspects.name: Corinne Vale
suspects.blurb: The understudy, ten years younger and two rungs down. The gallery decided it was her before the curtain hit the boards — who else profits from a dead diva?

suspects.name: Emil Sarto
suspects.blurb: The maestro. Feuded with Marchetti over tempi in front of the whole pit, twice. A man who conducts grudges con fuoco.

suspects.name: Tibor Kadar
suspects.blurb: Her dresser, twenty years in her service. Handles every prop, every costume, every bottle on that vanity — including the atomizer, every single night.

suspects.name: Aldous Wren
suspects.blurb: The production's backer, a silver-flask man with a box on the grand tier and a standing claim on the diva's Sundays. She was leaving his company for Chicago in the spring.

shards.text: Chemist: residue confined to the top chamber — one refill's worth. The bulb and lower well are clean. The poison went in late.
The maid: 'I misted my own throat from her atomizer at curtain, as she makes me do against tampering. I am standing here telling you about it.'
Stage manager's log: first interval, twenty-one minutes by the house clock, curtain to curtain.
Call-boy: the atomizer stood on the vanity at the interval bell — he saw it when he chalked the five-minute call on her door.
Police surgeon: the dose acted within minutes. She sprayed in the wings before her Act II entrance and was down before the cabaletta.
Carpet report: fresh crush marks before the vanity, heels together — a standing visitor, unhurried.
Wig-mistress: 'The powder I spilled at curtain was walked through once, by one pair of shoes, toes to the vanity.'
Doorman: room A's latch clicked twice in the interval — once in, once out. No third visit.
Corridor mirror line, house rule 11: one chalk mark issued in the interval — a grand-tier visitor, logged by weight of coat and no name asked.
Fingerprint man: the atomizer bulb wiped to a shine — not one print where every night there should be dozens.
Vanity glass: clean arcs through the powder film, the swipe of a gloved hand tidying after itself.
Found under the vanity lip: one grey kid glove-button, thread fresh-torn, the kind sewn at a gentleman's outfitter.
Cloak-room girl: a silver hip flask came back to its coat after the interval smelling of bitter almonds. She marked the tag from habit.
Chemist's swab of the flask neck: positive for prussic compound. The flask carried it; the atomizer received it.
The flask's monogram has been scratched out — fresh bright metal under the burr, done with a pocket blade, done lately.
Grand-tier barman: 'I filled no flask that night. House rule — and I would remember silver.'
Cloak-room tag ledger, interval column: the almond-ghost flask hung in coat-check 41 — the coat checked in by Aldous Wren's own man.
Sightline diagram: the dresser's bench sits square in the corridor mirror; the doorman watched Kadar's needle rise and fall the whole interval.
Wig-mistress: 'I sat opposite Tibor on the bench all twenty-one minutes, complaining about the third wig. He never left his seam.'
The mended Act II cloak: two hundred and fourteen stitches in a twenty-minute seam — counted by the wardrobe master, who pays by the stitch.
Call-boy: fetched Kadar grey thread at minute five and again at minute ten — 'he was on that bench both times, cross as a wet cat.'
Program, first interval: divertissement 'The Clockwork Garden' — C. Vale dances the mechanical nightingale before the curtain, in one.
Prompter's book: Vale's marks are continuous through the divertissement — she is never off the boards longer than a breath.
The Courier's critic, from his aisle seat: 'Miss Vale held the front of the curtain for the whole of the interval piece; I confess I timed her, having nothing kinder to do.'
Wardrobe: Vale is sewn into the nightingale pannier at the first-act curtain — a ten-minute job with two hands to cut her out.
The maestro's beat sheet, kept in pencil: cue marks for the nightingale run unbroken — 'V. present throughout, mercifully on the beat.'
Contract on file with the house manager: Vale to the Chicago Lyric for the spring season — signed and sealed three weeks BEFORE the poisoning.
Vale's agent, by wire: 'CHICAGO PAPERS CLOSED THE FIRST OF THE MONTH. SHE WAS COUNTING THE DAYS, NOT THE DIVA'S.'
House manager's countersignature and release: the understudy owed the Regina nothing after New Year's — the rung she is accused of killing for, she had already climbed past.
The entr'acte is Sarto's own arrangement, twenty-two minutes to the bar — he has never once let another stick touch it.
House recording disc, cut nightly for the archive: the entr'acte runs unbroken, one hand on the rubato throughout — his, by every man in the pit.
First desk, strings: 'The maestro never left the podium. We watch him for a living, monsieur.'
The harpist: 'He cued my page turns through the whole entr'acte, bar by bar, glaring, as is traditional.'
Box office runner: watched the pit from the rail the whole interval waiting on the receipts — 'the little maestro never stopped moving his arms.'
Medical card carried in Sarto's pocketbook: severe almond-compound allergy; exposure closes his throat within the minute.
Sarto's valet: 'I sniff his dressing room like a bloodhound each night. One macaroon in the flowers and we lose the maestro.'
Canteen standing order, posted since '45: no almond pastry in the artists' wing, by order of the management, for the maestro's sake.
The diva's standing orders, item nine: at each interval the atomizer stands untouched on the vanity — 'no hand but mine after curtain,' her oldest superstition.
Her maid: 'Tibor surrendered the spray to the vanity at first curtain, as he has done for twenty years. She would sack him for holding it one minute past.'
Key hook photograph: Kadar's key to room A hangs surrendered on the board at curtain, chalk-marked by the doorman — reclaimed only at the final bow.

contradictions.note: The same atomizer was harmless in the maid's throat at eight and lethal in the diva's at ten. The interval is the murder weapon.
Filled nowhere in the house, yet it came back to its coat with a bitter ghost. What the flask carried, it brought from outside — already mixed.

truth.motive: She was leaving him — the company, the box on the grand tier, the standing claim on her Sundays — for Chicago in the spring. Wren decided that if the Regina could not keep her, nothing would.
truth.summary: Aldous Wren poisoned the throat spray. The one chalk mark past the mirror line was his; the crushed carpet and gloved arcs at the vanity were his; the almond-ghost flask with the scratched monogram hung in his own man's coat-check; and the grey kid glove-button under the vanity lip lost its thread when he tidied up after himself.
reveal: The spray was clean at curtain — the maid misted her own throat from it and lived to say so.
The dresser never left his bench. The understudy never left the boards. The maestro never left the podium — and could not have carried that flask and finished the coda.
One visitor took the chalk past the mirror line: a grand-tier coat, no name asked.
The flask came back to coat-check 41 smelling of almonds, its monogram scratched to bright metal.
Coat-check 41 was Aldous Wren's. The glove-button under the vanity is grey kid, a gentleman's outfitter's work. She was leaving for Chicago; he made certain she left for nowhere.

facts.text: The atomizer was clean at curtain and poisoned during the twenty-one-minute first interval.
The poisoner stood gloved at the vanity in dressing room A during the interval.
The poison traveled in a silver hip flask that came back to the cloak room smelling of bitter almonds.
Kadar never crossed the mirror line — he sat the corridor bench, sewing, in full view.
Vale was on stage before the curtain for the entire interval divertissement.
Vale had already signed for Chicago — the motive the gallery gave her did not exist.
Sarto conducted his own entr'acte from the pit through the whole interval.
Sarto cannot come within a breath of almond compound and live to conduct the coda.
At intervals the atomizer sat outside Kadar's custody, on the vanity, by the diva's own standing order.
